tate of Califorllia 
air Political ractices Commissioll 
BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1 00 K STREET BUILDING, SACRMAENTO, 95814 
Mr. James Ruddick 
City Attorney 
212 Fourth street 
January 17, 1977 
Post Office Box 2410 
Marysville, California 
95901 
Dear Mr. Ruddick: 
/ 
This letter will confirm my telephone advice to you of 
January 10, 1977, and is in response to your letter of December 
17, 1976, requesting an informal opinion as to the status and 
potential conflicts of interest of Mr. L. G. Lamon, who was 
recently elected to the Marysville City Council. 
As you con-
firmed by telephone on January 10, 1977, and subsequently by 
letter executed by Mr. L. G. Lamon, your request for an 
informal opinion is made on behalf of Mr. Lamon. 
The Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended, does not 
prevent Mr. Lamon from serving as an elected City Councilman 
or as a member of the Redevelopment Agency because of his 
private financial interests. 
The Act does, however, require 
Mr. Lamon to disclose the existence of his private financial 
interests and, under appropriate circumstances, to disqualify 
himself from making or participating in the making of any 
governmental decisions as a member of the City Council and 
of the Redevelopment Agency. 
Thus, with regard to your 
question concerning status, Mr. Lamon is not prohibited 
from serving in his elected capacity_ 
His participation, 
however, may be limited. 
Government Code §87100 contains the bas 
interest prohibition and provides that: 
conflict of 
No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, 
ci-
pate in making or in any way attempt to 
use his official position to inf 
a governmental decision in which he knows 
or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest. 


James Ruddick 
January 17, 1977 
Page Two 
Government Code §87103 defines "financial interest": 
An official has a financial interest in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect 
on the public generally, on: 
(a) Any business entity in which the public 
official has a 
rect or indirect investment 
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); 
(b) Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); 
(c) Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the 
regular course of business, aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value 
received by or promised to the public official 
within twelve months prior to the time when 
the decision is made; or 
(d) Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management. 
For purposes of this section, 
indirect investment or interest means any 
investment or interest owned by the spouse 
or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, 
by 
any business entity controlled by the public 
official or by a trust in which he has a 
substantial interest. 
A business entity is 
controlled by a public official if the public 
official, his agents, spouse and dependent 
children hold more than fifty percent of the 
ownership interest in the entity. 
A public 
official has a substantial interest in a trust 
when the official, 
s spouse and dependent 
children have a present or future interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 


James Ruddick 
January 17, 1977 
Page Three 
In your letter you state that "because of the relatively 
small size of the Marysville Plaza Project, almost any action 
taken by the Marysville Redevelopment Agency would have an 
impact upon the value of Mr. Lamon's property, and in all 
probability, in many cases, there would be a material finan-
cial impact". 
On the basis of these asserted facts, it seems 
clear that the real 
purchased by Mr. Lamon, which is 
within the Marysville Redeve 
t Area, would constitute a 
"financial interest" within the meaning of Government Code 
§87100. 
Accordingly, it seems highly probable that Mr. Lamon 
will be required to disquali 
himself from making or parti-
cipating in making many of the decisions required to be made 
by the Redevelopment 
For more specificity regarding 
what constitutes "making or partic 
ing in making a govern-
mental decision", I refer you to 2 Cal. Adm. Code §§18700(b) 
and (c) I 
a copy of which I attach. 
For more precise guidance 
in determining when a decision will have a "material" effect 
on a financial interest, I refer you to 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
§§18702(a) and (b). 
Subsection (a) requires an evaluation 
as to whether l under all the circumstances and facts 
known to the public offi al at the time of the decision, 
the existence of the official's f 
al interest might 
interfere with the making of an impartial de 
sion. 
Sub-
section (b) provides a more 
ective test based upon an 
evaluation of the financial impact of the decision on the 
official's interest. 
Absent 
provision of more specific 
facts concerning the property 
by Mr. Lamon within the 
Marysville Redevelopment Area, and the specific types of 
decisions to be made by the Agency, I am unable to respond 
with speci 
city to the particular types of decisions to be 
made by the Agency on which Mr. Lamon would be required to 
disqualify himself. 
It seems appropriate, briefly, to point out the sanctions 
applicable to violation of a disqualification requirement. 
Government Code §87102 limits the sanctions available for 
violation of §87100 to i 
unctive relief. 
Violation of the 
disqualification requirements of a Con 
ict of Interest Code, 
however, subjects one to all of the criminal and civil sanc-
tions contained in Ch 
11, beginning with Government Code 
§91000 of the Act. 
As a member of the 
lle Ci 
Council, 
Mr. Lamon is subje t 
sions, includ 
the disqual-
ification provisions, of the Ci 
Council's Conflict of Interest 
Code. 
Accordingly, all of the criminal and civil sanctions 
conta 
in the Act wou 
be 
icable to Mr. Lamon for 
alation of 
disqua ification requirement 
in the 
Marysvil 
Ci 
Counc l' 
Confl 
0 
I 


James Ruddick 
January 17, 1977 
Page Four 
You inquire, additionally, as to any addi 
onal 
difficulties that Mr. Lamon would encounter as chairman 
of the Redevelopment Agency. 
The prohibition contained 
in ~overnment Code §87100 extends to a public official 
who in any way may "attempt to use his offic 
1 position 
to influence a governmental decision in 
ch he knows 
or has reason to know he has a financial interest". 
While the precise powers of the ch 
rman of your 
Redevelopment Agency are not clear from the by-laws 
of the Agency, normal ,a chairman has ample power, 
by scheduling items on the agenda, calling and recog-
nizing witnesses, permitt 
, encouraging, or discour-
aging testimony, etc., to influence a governmental 
decision. 
Thus, if Mr. Lamon were to serve as chairman 
of your Agency, it seems highly 
kely that he would be 
required to disqualify himself from making a great number 
of decisions that a chairman normally makes, to avoid 
breach of this §87100 proh 
ition. 
As you indicated by telephone that you already have 
copies of the Owen and Thorner opinions, I attach only a 
copy of the AntiOCh opinion (No. 75-083), which you may 
find of some assistance in reviewing the facts in your 
case. 
I hope that these informal remarks are of some 
assistance to you in resolving the issues raised in your 
letter. 
Please let me know if I can be of further assis-
tance to you. 
EAK:glb 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
~'~7 
ErA. Kerrt'~ 
S aff Attorney 
Conflicts of Interest 
Division 


KEELEY, GUTHRIE l1 RU DDICK 
TERENCE l. KEELEY 
JOEL T. GUTHRJE 
JAMES M. RUDDICK 
MICHAEL C. FITZPATRICK. 
ATTORNEYS AT LAW 
P. O. BOX 2410 
212 FOU RTH STREET 
MAR.YSVf LLE, CALI FOR.N fA 9590 I 
December 17, 1976 
Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Jr. 
Chief Conflicts of Interest Division 
Fair Political Practice Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Mr. Spurlock: 
TELEPHONE 
C916: 743-5471 
At the recommendation of Mr. Ed Carey of your office, I am 
writing this letter to request an informal opinion regarding 
a potential conflict of interest facing a councilman-elect 
of the City of Marysville. 
I am writing this letter as City 
Attorney and am doing so at the request of Mayor Frank 
Comarsh. 
At the election of November 2, 1976, the voters of the City 
of Marysville elected three new councilmen, one of whom was 
Mr. L. G. Lamon. 
In addition to action as a councilman for 
the City of Marysville, Mr. Lamon will be a member of the 
Marysville Community Development Agency (the Redevelopment 
Agency operating under the provisions of §§33000, et seq, 
of the Health and Safety Code). 
Under the terms of the 
Charter of the City of Marysville, Mr. Lamon will be sworn 
in as a councilman (and, therefore, as a member of the 
Marysville Community Development Agency) on January 3, 1977. 
Since his election, Mr. Lamon has purchased certain real prop-
erty within the Marysville Redevelopment Area, which area is 
commonly designated as the Marysville Plaza project. 
Had 
Mr. Lamon purchased this real property after taking office, 
he would have done so in violation of §33l30 of the Health 
and Safety Code which prohibits the acquisition of property 
in a project area by any agency personnel. 
However, since 
he purchased the interest in the real property prior to 
actually becoming a councilman, I believe that the only 
requirement of §33l30 is that he make a written disclosure 
of his interest to the agency. 


Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Jr. 
Page two 
December 17, 1976 
The problems which concern all of us are probably obvious. 
Because of the relatively small size of the Marysville Plaza 
Project, almost any action taken by the Marysville Community 
Development Agency would have an impact upon the value of 
Mr. Lamon's property, and in all probability, in many cases, 
there would be a material financial impact. 
As indicated 
above, I am therefore requesting an informal opinion from 
the Fair Political Practice Commission in which it considers 
Mr. Lamon's status and the potential of a conflict of interest 
arising from various actions in which he might participate as 
a member of the Marysville Community Development Agency. 
In 
order to assist you in your analysis of this matter, I have 
enclosed the following: 
1. 
A statement from Mr. Dennis Matthews, Director 
of the Marysville Community Development Agency, 
in which he gives a resume of the history as 
well as future development plans of the Agency. 
2. 
A copy of the Marysville Plaza Project Plan 
and a report thereon. 
3. 
A copy of the Ordinance adopting the Marysville 
Plaza Project. 
4. 
A copy of the Ordinance in which the City Council 
declared itself to be the Redevelopment Agency 
in accordance with §33200 of the Health and 
Safety Code. 
In addition to considering the potential conflicts of interest 
which Mr. Lamon will face as a member of the Marysville 
Community Development Agency, I would also appreciate it if 
you would include in your opinion a consideration of what 
additional difficulties would be encountered if Mr. Lamon 
were to serve as Chairman of the Agency. 
In order to assist 
you in an analysis of that aspect of the problem, I have also 
enclosed a copy of the By-Laws pursuant to which the Agency 
conducts business. 
It would seem possible that even if Mr. 
Lamon were to abstain from voting on most Agency matters, his 
exercise of the duties of Chairman of the Agency alone could 
be deemed as participation in the decision-making and could 
therefore create a conflict of interest. 


Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Jr. 
Page three 
December 17, 1976 
In addition to the informal opinion which you render in 
response to this request, I would appreciate it if you 
would also send to me copies of any decisions which the 
Fair Political Practice Commission has issued to date 
dealing with potential conflicts of interest faced by 
redevelopment agency personnel from other jurisdictions. 
As indicated above, Mr. Lamon will be sworn in as a City 
Councilman on January 3, 1977. 
The first meeting of the 
Marysville Community Development Agency will not be held 
until January 10, 1977. 
Accordingly, if it is at all 
possible, I would appreciate it if your informal opinion 
could be rendered somewhat in advance of the January 10 
meeting. 
If you need any additional information with regard to the 
Marysville Community Development Agency or its activities, 
please feel free to call me. 
In many instances, if questions 
deal with the nature of future plans and the time table there-
fore, it may be more expeditious for you to call Mr. Dennis 
Matthews, the Director of the Agency. 
He can be reached at 
743-2001. 
Thank you for your kind attention to this matter. 
JMR:cf 
Encls. 
Very truly yours, 
JAMES M. RUDDICK 
City Attorney of the 
City of Marysville 
cc: 
Mr. Dennis Matthews (No encls.) 
Mr. Frank Comarsh (No encls.)

sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act of 1974 does not prevent Mr. Lamon from serving as an elected City Councilman or as a member of the Redevelopment Agency because of his private financial interests. The Act does, however, require Mr. Lamon to disclose the existence of his private financial interests and, under appropriate circumstances, to disqualify himself from making or participating in the making of any governmental decisions. It seems highly probable that Mr. Lamon will be required to disqualify himself from making or participating in making many of the decisions required to be made by the Redevelopment Agency. If Mr. Lamon were to serve as chairman, he would likely be required to disqualify himself from making a great number of decisions that a chairman normally makes, to avoid breach of Government Code §87100 prohibitions.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry concerns whether a newly elected city councilman and redevelopment agency member can serve in these positions given his ownership of real property in the redevelopment project area, and what additional conflicts arise if he serves as agency chairman.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Lamon may serve in both positions but must disclose his financial interests and recuse himself from decisions where those interests are materially affected; serving as chairman would create substantially more disqualification requirements due to the chairman's influence over agency decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated December 17, 1976) and FPPC response letter (dated January 17, 1977). Extraction focused on FPPC response. OCR quality issues present but content extractable. Question and conclusion sections identified and quoted verbatim from response letter.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act of 1974 does not prevent Mr. Lamon from serving as an elected City Councilman or as a member of the Redevelopment Agency because of his private financial interests. The Act does, however, require Mr. Lamon to disclose the existence of his private financial interests and, under appropriate circumstances, to disqualify himself from making or participating in the making of any governmental decisions. It seems highly probable that Mr. Lamon will be required to disqualify himself from making or participating in making many of the decisions required to be made by the Redevelopment Agency. If Mr. Lamon were to serve as chairman, he would likely be required to disqualify himself from making a great number of decisions that a chairman normally makes, to avoid breach of Government Code §87100 prohibitions.

Thus, with regard to your question concerning status, Mr. Lamon is not prohibited from serving in his elected capacity_ His participation, however, may be limited. Government Code §87100 contains the bas interest prohibition and provides that: conflict of No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, ci- pate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to inf a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. James Ruddick January 17, 1977 Page Two Government Code §87103 defines "financial interest": An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a rect or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, by any business entity controlled by the public official
embedding.summary: FPPC advises that an elected city councilman and redevelopment agency member with property interests in a project area must comply with the Political Reform Act's disclosure and disqualification requirements, with particularly stringent limitations if serving as agency chairman.
